State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission

P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance Administration • • • Executive Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5660          322-5901          322-6441          322-6444

June 26, 1978

H.P. (Hal) Shawlee
Manager, Civic Affairs
Union Oil Co. of California
Union Oil Center
P.O. Box 7600
Los Angeles, CA 90015

Dear Hal:

Thank you for your letter of May 22, 1978,
regarding filing of campaign statements. My apologies
for not answering you sooner; the June Primary has kept
us very busy.

Initially, I agree with you that something must
be done to reduce the number of places and the number of
times you are filing reports. Although the best solution
is to consolidate all elections on a March, June, November
basis, I recognize that each city for some reason wants
to keep its separate date. With Proposition 13, however,
the consolidation of elections, which clearly reduces
costs, should be much more attractive.

This summer our staff will be looking at all the
campaign disclosure provisions and regulations with a view
to making the Act easier to follow. I am forwarding your
letter to Lee Rosenthal who will be in charge of the review.

I don't see any problem with allowing major donors
to file monthly reports (other than the 2 pre-election
reports before the June and November elections), but I do
see a problem with allowing committees which raise money
and give to several candidates (e.g., United for California,
legislator controlled committees) filing monthly reports.
It's my view that the public will miss some valuable informa-
tion in the latter cases.

Three points about your particular filing. It is

H.P. (Hal) Shawlee
June 26, 1978
Page Two


not clear to me why you filed in Fresno or Orange Counties.
From your campaign statements that you sent me, it does
not appear that you were required to do so.

     Second, why did you file a one day statement for
Long Beach? If your contribution to Hall was the only
contribution you were going to make in Long Beach, you could
have designated your first pre-election statement filed in
Long Beach a "final statement" and avoided all further filings
there. You can do that with all the local jurisdictions and
save some time and effort.

     Finally, why send your April 24 statement to all
jurisdictions? Why not file with Long Beach (if you have to)
and then file with the state office 12 days before the elec-
tion using April 24 as the beginning date.

     Let's discuss this further, if you are in Sacramento,
drop in so we can exchange some more ideas. Our goal is the
same; we should be able to agree on a solution to the problem.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        Robert M. Stern
                                        General Counsel

BS:tb
cc: Lee Rosenthal

Union Oil Company of California

Union Oil Center, P.O. Box 7600, Los Angeles, California 9005
Telephone (213) 486-6446

FPPC
MAY 24 9 18 AM '78

union
76

H. P. (Hal) Shawlee
Manager, Civic Affairs

May 22, 1978

Mr. Robert Stern
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Dear Bob:

When I saw you on February 21, I gave you a copy of a letter I had written some
time ago explaining the difficulty of making the numerous reports if an organiza-
tion supports a number of candidates in a number of different elections.

The attached material is further proof of that difficulty.

"Report #1" is the First Pre-election Report for the June 6 primary and is
self-explanatory. The Distribution Sheet shows all the agencies we had to cover.
The notes to Sacramento County, Simi Valley, and Los Angeles City were required
because we had previously reported a contribution for elections within their
jurisdictions.

"Report #2"is the Pre-election Report for the Long Beach Special Runoff of May 9
that had a closing date of April 24, one day following the closing date for
"Report #1". We made no contributions during that one-day period but had to make
the report and send it to the entire Distribution Sheet as indicated.

The multiplicity of reports is incurred only by those companies, unions, or
organizations that support a number of candidates in a number of different
elections. With primaries on different dates and some of those primaries having
runoffs, it is extremely difficult to keep track of all the closing dates. We
are required to send copies to every jurisdiction that received a previous report
so that there are no gaps of reported time.

The Federal Election Commission recognized a similar problem on a national level
with the different primary dates of the several states. The FEC now permits
Multicandidate Committees to report monthly from January through September and
then use the regular pre-election and post-election reporting schedule for the
general.

Mr. Robert Stern
May 22, 1978
Page Two


Is it possible that the Fair Political Practices Commission could adopt some
schedule that might be more realistic?

Incidentally, since I have mentioned this matter previously to Assemblyman Paul
Priolo, I have sent him a similar letter with attachments.

Sincerely,

[signature]

:jc

Attachments

Form 461

EXPENDITURE AND MAJOR DONOR
COMMITTEE CAMPAIGN STATEMENT

(Government Code S ction 84200-84216)

officeholder or committee; or, (2) persi who have made independent expendi-
tions on behalf of a candidate, officei or by ballot measure in the amount of
$500 or more.

sections.question_synthetic: Union Oil Company seeks guidance on simplifying the burden of filing multiple campaign disclosure reports across different jurisdictions when supporting numerous candidates in elections with different closing dates and primary schedules.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is primarily incoming request/correspondence, not an FPPC advice letter response. Contains formal letter from H.P. Shawlee (Union Oil) to Robert Stern (FPPC Counsel) dated May 22, 1978, plus attached Form 461 campaign disclosure statements. No QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections typical of FPPC advice letters. References letter previously sent to FPPC on February 21, 1978, and mentions similar communication to Assemblyman Paul Priolo. The document illustrates the procedural problem being raised but does not contain FPPC's response/advice.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: Union Oil Company seeks guidance on simplifying the burden of filing multiple campaign disclosure reports across different jurisdictions when supporting numerous candidates in elections with different closing dates and primary schedules.

Box 7600 Los Angeles, CA 90015 Dear Hal: Thank you for your letter of May 22, 1978, regarding filing of campaign statements. My apologies for not answering you sooner; the June Primary has kept us very busy. Initially, I agree with you that something must be done to reduce the number of places and the number of times you are filing reports. Although the best solution is to consolidate all elections on a March, June, November basis, I recognize that each city for some reason wants to keep its separate date. With Proposition 13, however, the consolidation of elections, which clearly reduces costs, should be much more attractive. This summer our staff will be looking at all the campaign disclosure provisions and regulations with a view to making the Act easier to follow. I am forwarding your letter to Lee Rosenthal who will be in charge of the review. I don't see any problem with allowing major donors to file monthly reports (other than the 2 pre-election reports before the June and November elections), but I do see a problem with allowing committees which raise money and give to several candidates (e.g., United for California, legislator controlled committees) filing monthly reports. It's my view that the public will miss some valuable informa- tion in the latter cases. Three points about your particular filing. It is H.P. (Hal) Shawlee June 26, 1978 Page Two not clear to me why you filed in Fresno or Orange Counties. From your campaign statements that you sent me, it does not appear that you were required to do so. Second, why did you file a one day statement for Long Beach? If your contribution to Hall was the only contribution you were going to make in Long Beach, you could have designated your first pre-election statement filed in Long Beach a "final statement" and avoided all further filings there. You can do that with all the local jurisdictions and save some time and effort. Finally, why send your April 24 statement to all jurisdictions? Why not file with Long Beach (if you have to) and then file with the state office 12 days before the elec- tion using April 24 as the beginning date. Let's discuss this further, if you are in Sacramento, drop in so we can exchange some more ideas. Our goal is the same; we should be able to agree on a solution to the problem. embedding.summary: This document consists of incoming correspondence from Union Oil Company of California requesting the FPPC consider adopting a more realistic reporting schedule to address the difficulty of filing multiple disclosure reports (Form 461) across numerous jurisdictions when supporting candidates in multiple elections with staggered primary and runoff dates. The company includes sample reports and distribution sheets as evidence of the reporting burden.
